The reality is kahit yung mga sinasabing nakaahon mula sa kahirapan ay mahirap pa rin kung tutuusin The Philippine government defines poor as a household of 5 making less than 12k pesos per month These are destitute people so when someone transitions to low income 12k to 24k or lower middle income 24k to 48k monthly feeling nila na nakaahon na sila sa hirap Nothing can be further from the truth IBON sets the monthly living wage for a family of 5 at 26k monthly That s the bare minimum for a decent standard of living By every conceivable metric this is poor For a household of five living a middle class lifestyle in the city even a six digit earner can live paycheck to paycheck Nevermind having an emergency fund that can last 6 months to a year Nevermind having NOTHING saved for retirement Nevermind having a portfolio that can serve as a hedge against inflation Pero for so so many success na ito

Conyo is like the valley girl accent of the Philippines. Conyo sounds obnoxious, Taglish doesn't. If you don't sound obnoxious while mixing English and Filipino, you're probably speaking in Taglish. Taglish is also more respectful of grammar. For example: Taglish: In my opinion, medyo overrated ang McDo, so let's eat at Chowking instead. Mas masarap pa naman yung pagkain doon. Conyo: You know, McDo is like, so overrated. Let's make kain sa Chowking instead. The food's more delicious pa naman doon.

Hindi ito human nature, ito ay na-program sa atin ng systema. Relihiyon at gobyerno ang mga instrumento ng brainwashing. Nung panahon ng Mesopotamia, akala nila sentro sila ng mundo. Hanggang ngayon, ganon pa rin tayo sa maraming paraan. Christianity ang nagpakalat ng afterlife beliefs bilang tool ng oppression. Problema: ang mga institusyong pang-edukasyon naka-angkla pa rin sa religious orders. Walang tunay na secular Enlightenment Period ang nangyari dito. Religiosity breeds entitlement, 'Chosen ako ng Diyos.' Kaya nagboboto tayo ng mali, nananatiling mahirap, nag-iimbak ng yaman dahil 'will ng Diyos.' Kailangan ng reset.

Huwag i-blame ang relihiyon palagi, pero tingnan mo ang estadistika. Ang mga 'irreligious' na bansa tulad ng Netherlands at Sweden ay mas maayos. Yung North Korea, China, Cuba? Hindi sila tunay na irreligious, may state religion, Buddhism, at animism pa rin. Ang punto: kahit walang invisible na diyos, pwede pa ring mag-exist ang fanaticism, mga taong nag-worship kay Hitler, o sa mga political idol. Kahit kailan pinagsama-sama ang suspension of logic at fanatical belief, nagkakagulo tayo. Case in point: Amerika. Case in point: Pilipinas.

Not exactly the saddest moment but when I found out about the Master Plan to control flooding of southern part of metro manila (Paranaque, muntinlupa and pasay), designed by [PERSON], but it was not completed because of "1986". Apparently only one more phase was not completed, the outlet of laguna de bay going to pacific ocean. So now water from rizal province and those around laguna de bay just fill up the lake until it overflows to the surrounding areas. I found this out last July when heavy rains caused flooding and eventually entered our house to just around a foot high causing water damage to our furniture and appliances. We were lucky because water subsided after around an hour and only a few of our things got damaged beyond repair, most just needed cleaning. I cannot say the same to some of our neighbors and nearby areas where flooding actually required them to evacuate in the middle of a pandemic.

Wide sidewalks, mixed zoning, compact industrial zones, tramways, underground PNR na hindi nagco-cross ng bawat kalye sa Manila. Nationwide freeway system. Underground electric lines. Filipino-oriental-Spanish architecture bilang identity. Mas maraming paaralan na malayo sa downtown, tulad ng Cornell na nasa gitna ng wala, o UP Los Baños. Passenger and cargo trains nationwide para mabawasan ang trucks sa daan. Government bus transit na owned ng estado. Ang pinaka-hadlang lang: politikong nag-iinvest lang sa bagay na magpapababa sa kanilang pangalan at pera.

The Philippines is a hopeless case because Filipinos are the problem, and there is no sign that this will change anytime soon. We always blame the government for our situation and the rampant corruption, but we forget who operates the government. Yes—Filipinos. We are corrupt in our own little ways, under the guise of “diskarte.” We take advantage of small things and even feel proud of it, but little do we know that we are always ready to exploit opportunities whenever they present themselves, even if it means stealing or inconveniencing others. We also lack discipline. We constantly advocate for change, yet we complain whenever someone initiates change if it doesn’t align with our personal agendas. Because of this, changing the Philippines is difficultas Filipinos themselves are hard to deal with. We always focus on the negative. It’s always “damned if you do, damned if you don’t". We want change, until it interferes with our personal interests—then we oppose it

Napansin ko sa mga Pilipino, destructive ang karamihan ng criticism. Hindi lang sinesabihan ka ng mali, binabago pa ang confidence mo nang wala man lang suggestions. Tumaba ako pagkatapos ng college dahil sa stress eating at nahirapan akong ibalik. Tapos paligid-ligid, memes tungkol sa mataba, mga taong walang pakialam na nagsasalita ng fat jokes, palayaw na related sa katawan. Nakakababa ng loob. Kahit yung 'Ang taba mo na, mag-diet ka', may suggested action pero para sa akin mocked pa rin. Kasi parang lahat ng pumupuna sa weight ko ay tawanan lang. Kaya pag may nagko-comment sa akin tungkol dito, sobrang offended talaga.

Gladly, nainvolved ako sa mga tao na same sa sitwasyon ko na open minded sa mga ganung criticism at ginawa nilang motivation yun to change/improve. Naadopt ko yung way of thinking nila then inexpose ko yung sarili ko sa mga ganung community, na nag ki criticize sa bad effects ng pagiging overweight at nagbibigay ng tips and advice on how to lose weight. Then dun ko naumpisahang tanggapin yung criticism na natatanggap ko padin sa time na yun, triny ko mag bawas ng timbang. Kaya sa environment din talaga na ginagalawan mo. Having lived in Thailand and the Philippines, I can honestly say that there is quite a difference. It's not a personal issue, but it gets under your skin after a while. Where nowhere is utopia you definitely get a better deal in Thailand. Customer service, quality, choice of products, infrastructure, transportation and dare I say it common sense. The Philippines is withheld from growing and developing. Due in part to dynastic influence, poor education and a lackluster approach to do better. Yes they are an archipelago so you understand that it's not as easy to travel, but the travel experience is nearly always dirty and uncomfortable. Could do better. The choice of products is controlled by San Miguel Corporation, if they don't make it, it's difficult to get. Education, when the kids are in school is at best to an elementary standard. Singing and dancing tends to be popular on the curriculum, which has a place but not a mainstay. The approach to most things is to not be punctual, it's acceptable and almost a national pride in being tardy. This in itself is a huge downfall and a poor reflection.

Karamihan ng NATO member states ay positibo sa NATO dahil naranasan nila ang Soviet Union. Ang US ay hindi pa sumasakop ng kahit anong European country. Si Russia naman ay sumaboy sa Ukraine, Chechnya, at Georgia. Walang bansang forced na sumali sa NATO, pwedeng umalis kahit kailan. Ang France at Greece ay nag-iwan noon ng military command pero bumalik ng kusa. Para maging member, kailangang matugunan ang political, economic, at military standards, at approved ng lahat ng kasalukuyang miyembro. Ang NATO ay hindi coercive, ito ay voluntary. Romanticization of the family. No matter how toxic, shitty, or abusive, they tell you “pamilya pa rin si (name)” or “tatay/nanay/kapatid/lolo/lola mo pa rin yan” like it’s a free pass for everything. It leaves them unaccountable and perpetuates the toxicity and abuse in our culture. If we want better, we need to be better and that includes holding the people we love and care about accountable for their actions. Enabling isn’t loving. Enabling isn’t supporting. You set people up for failure when you prevent them from taking responsibility for their actions and humanity.

Smart-shaming is the term I’ve heard before, and I don’t know if it’s something that’s common in the Philippines. My nephew told me that he’s really hesitant to speak English around others and me because he doesn’t want to be seen as pretentious or overly smart since he’s in a tough financial situation. It’s the same from other family members who have told me about similar situations they experienced. My nephew speaks proficient English, much better than me, a native English speaker. I told him not to worry about speaking English, because it’s relatively flexible and I’ll still understand what he means. I also speak two “forms” of English, a “professional” English meant for work only, so that’s really formal and structured properly, and “Dun Gud” English, which is much more casual and relaxed. Usually that’s around friends, family, and when I’m off duty. I don’t know if smart-shaming is a thing in the Philippines, but I’ve been told that it exists by family.

Ngayon, mahaba na naman ang pila sa ospital, at wala pa ring bagong batas para ayusin ang bilang ng staff. Tinangkilik natin ang resilience hanggang naging rason na ito para hindi na tayo kumilos.

I genuinely loathe the hiring practices in the Philippines. It seems that many companies here can be quite exploitative, expecting one person to manage a wide range of responsibilities. For instance, a job posting for a graphic designer might also require expertise in marketing strategy, social media management, SEO analysis, audio and video editing, and sound mixing etc. This is literally already one team and they want one person to handle all of this. In contrast, I've noticed that job requirements in other countries often focus on just two key qualifications: a relevant diploma and relevant experience. It's disheartening to see that many of my peers endure significant exploitation and relentless exhaustion. I've encountered comments from older individuals who label me as naive for questioning the status quo, as they claim "that's just how it's always been." However, I express my concern because I believe in the potential for improvement. My experience working with international clients has shown me that specialization is valued over generalization, and I know that a better approach is possible.

And take note, two separate groups, all asking for info about me. Di nalang nila ako tinanong diretso, dinaan pa yung pagkachismoso/chismosa nila sa mga friends ko. My friends were confused and gave them vague answers. And the look they gave me finally clicked, maybe they realized I was living a good life lol So I guess yung out of line na curiosity

It's agitating how a small-time power figure manages to get away with this. No court proceedings, no investigation started — this kid and others are being harassed and assaulted yet the perpetrator walks free. It's only a kagawad's son and he commits something so heinous and escapes accountability — that shows how corrupt and broken our system is. And this girl is one hell of a genius. She's in a terrible environment — third world country run by sellouts, abused in multiple ways — yet she earned honors and wrote one of the best letters I've ever read from someone her age. It puts my classmates to shame. I genuinely hope more blessings come her way and that she gets the justice she deserves. I want that guy to rot in jail. If anyone has updates please share — this story motivated me to get back into journalism.

Nakatira ako doon at totoo, walang pila na mala-MRT sa SM North EDSA o Ortigas. Ang reklamo ng iba, may mga lines na hindi sulit kasi hindi napupuno. Pero kahit sa Japan siksikan din pag rush hour, at may kamote riders din. Bihira lang yung tipong papasok sa right exit tapos sisingit mula pinakakaliwang lane tulad ng nangyayari dito. Grab at taxi mas marunong pumila sa general. Mayroon silang train line from airport to city center — tayo wala. At hindi mo masasabi na sing-traffic lang doon kumpara sa atin kung hindi ka pa nakapunta. Pinakamasaklap, iniwan talaga tayo ng gobyerno pagdating sa public transport infrastructure. Yung taong nagsasabing mas malala pa raw ang ibang bansa kesa sa atin — bobo lang talaga, amputa. Nakakadismaya ang kalagayan ng ating sistema.

Kapag bumoboto ang mga Pilipino, sa kulay at pamilya sila tumitingin — yon ang nakakalungkot. Matitigas ang ulo — naranasan mo na ang administrasyon ng tatay, susubukan mo pa rin ang anak. Cycle na sobrang hirap basagin. Madaling malinlang ang mga Pilipino sa mga flowery words at pangakong too good to be true naman. Baby pa lang ako, problema na yang transportation — wala namang pinagbago. Mga modern jeep, ganoon pa rin: punuan, tayuan, mainit. LRT at MRT lagi pa ring siksikan at mahaba ang pila. Wala pa ring maayos na PUV stops, wala pa ring maayos na sidewalk. Walang ikaayos ang Pilipinas kung paulit-ulit na mapapalinlang ang mga tao sa mga korap na pulitiko at color box politics. Mga politiko na yan, hindi sumubok gumamit ng public transpo sa araw-araw — sakay lang pag may kasamang media, para sa optics. Baka kailangan ko pang magkaapo bago umayos ang public transport natin.

Nakakainis tlaga. Whenever i travel, aamaze ako sa transpo system ng ibang bansa tapos di ko maiwasang mainis sa gobyerno naten. Gusto ko yung sinasabi mong makatao — kasi clearly hindi ganun sa atin. Pagod ka na sa work, pipila ka pa ng ganun kahaba sa MRT, siksikan pa pagpasok. Wala nang work-life balance ang Pinoy, ganyan pa ang transpo naten. Sa ibang bansa, kahit rush hour, efficient at hassle-free ang subway. Maiisip mo talaga kung gaano tayo ginago ng gobyerno over the decades. Pati sa energy, napakamahal ng Meralco — wala silang heart para gumawa ng paraan. Bahala tayong magbayad ng mahal, basta hindi sila nag-invest sa alternative energy. Hinayaan nilang lamunin ng malalaking corporation ang Edsa. Sa Korea, kahit malaki ang mall along the road, hindi nito nasasakop ang kalsada. Kaya ordinaryong Pilipino nahihirapan sa Edsa. Nakakadisappoint talaga.

Sobrang predictable na yan. Mga batang scholars o hindi, nakikita nila ang revolution bilang instant meaning sa buhay. Problema lang, ang global success rate ng protracted people's war na concept ng NPA ay less than 2% ever na nagresulta sa sustainable reform. Historical pattern — Russia, China, Cuba, Venezuela — halos walang nanalo. Studies sa Asia Pacific show na 60-70% ng ex-rebels succeed sa stable employment within 3-5 years with proper support systems. Pinakapractical na landas ay hindi ang bundok — yung ideology na yan 50 taon nang patay sa global scale. Leaders abroad comfortable habang followers sa bundok parang cannon fodder. Sa Mindoro girl na yan, isinabak nila sa front line kahit estudyante lang. Sa atin, may TESDA at DILG reintegration programs pero walang accountability ang mga recruiters kaya paulit-ulit na may nabubudol na kabataan. Bottom line: reform, education, legal avenues ang solusyon — hindi bundok at baril. Protect the kids, educate, then integrate. Reform not rebellion.

Hindi ko maintindihan kung bakit lagi na lang privatization ang sagot ng mga tao. People see it succeeding in other countries and assume it will work here too — but we only see the successes, not the failures. Privatization doesn't automatically mean better; the private entity has to be competent din. Look at LRT 2 — fully government owned, and halos kalahati ng original 2004 trains ay hindi na gumagana. May speed restrictions na dahil sa inoperative signaling. Elevators at escalators noon hindi gumagana ng years. People see this and conclude: wag na ipahawak sa gobyerno. Then congressmen defund maintenance kasi 'di naman maganda ang pamamalakad.' The result: performance drops further. It's a bad cycle — minsan kailangan nating tanggapin na more government funding is the real solution, hindi necessarily privatization. TL;DR — people don't respect the fine details and nuances ng isyung ito.

The name [PERSON] is of Hebrew and Egyptian origin, most commonly meaning "exalted," "strong," or "mountain of strength". Having kids is a blessing. However, ang pagtataguyod at pagsuporta sa bata ay mabigat na responsibilidad. I hate the mentality na kelangan mag anak para may makasama sa pagtanda or para may magsuporta sa magulang. Never dapat naging "investment" ang anak. You want to bring a child to this world dahil emotionally, mentally, physically, and most of all financially ready ka. Sa panahon ngayon na napaka uncertain din ng future, it is wise na pag isipang mabuti ang mga bagay bagay dahil kawawa ang batang hindi nasusuportahan ng maayos sa lahat ng needs nya. Kung nagdaan sa matinding hirap, lesson learned yun pagdating sa buhay pamilya. Still, salute sa lahat ng magigiting na parents na araw araw nagsusumikap para lang maitaguyod ang pamilya. Kids don't deserve a crappy and miserable life kaya take your time to build yourself and your finances before raising one.

This was so funny because it turned out that the dead fish she replaced was literally just a feeder fish. For those who don’t know, in the aquarium hobby, some of us keep large carnivorous fishes that aren’t content to eat commercial fish pellets nor flakes. They need to be fed with other fishes; we call these “feeder fishes”. They’re usually just common goldfish that are super duper cheap (around Php 1-4 lang for each one).
Ito ba yung sa goldfish? Then it turns out, pagkain pala ng totoong alagang fish yung gold fish.

I have a few. Pero to start with, today. I feel happy knowing it's been exactly a year since umalis ako sa previous job ko. I was overworked but underpaid yet ngayon I'm happy na I have enough working remotely. Last month, natapos ko na bayaran yung biggest debt ko, and I'm down to my last one. Bukas naman, isang taon na ako dito sa province na kasama ang parents at kapatid ko. I'm happy na magkakasama na kami sa wakas after I ended my 6 years relationship of abuse and decided to leave the city and be back home. I don't have kids (see: username) but I would probably do straight English, straight Tagalog, and a foreign language. Any of the Asian languages will do. They learn languages fast when they're young and speaking a third language would be a great advantage forever. I don't think you have to teach them Taglish, it comes naturally.

Why not household - Tagalog, Outside - English? This is to promote the Filipino Language as well as building confidence in English Language. Sayang din kasi, ayoko matulad na nagsisimulang mawala na language sa province namen, Kapampangan. EDIT: This is for our culture din kasi.
I'm planning to send him to an english speaking school given the chance we love their curriculum and their way of teaching. Thanks for your share.
Well we're not sure if the parents are proficient in Filipino. But you're right, just make the resources for learning available to the kid and let the kid learn on his own.
just get him hooked on disney or download some classic disney movies. for sure hell be speaking english in no time and some values too. :D
Speak the language enough and you become more confident with it. But for kids, the mere derision by peers might easily affect this confidence.

For me, children nowadays are so easy to learn on how to speak English because of social medias like youtube. And it is kinda common to children too nowadays that they speak english. But it is also important that they will not forget their native language.

If your kid is not surrounded with strong English speakers, don't. It's harder to correct grammar than teach it. Teach him/her Filipino first. Then encourage watching Disney and read them English story books. Nothing can teach better grammar than great love for reading award winning books.

Both, but speak in straight English or Filipino. Childhood is best for language acquisition and this will set them up best for school, ordinary interaction, and everything else life will throw at them. If you can, throw in a foreign languages as well. It's amazing how well they pick up the quirks and little nuances of language. If your child interacts fairly often with the rest of your family you can divide up the language-teaching duties. My sister and her side of the family (city folk) often speak to my nephews in English and my brother-in-law and his side (probinsyanos) Tagalog.

Parent of a 9 y.o. kid who decided to choose English as his primary language when he was 3 because he was confused with using either Hiligaynon(my native language) or Tagalog(his dad's). Teaching the child to use all English or all Filipino when conversing is the best way(IMO) because Taglish is really building poor communication skills and it's making kids lazy by not learning the appropriate words to use.

Huwag pilitin ang bata mag-English exclusively. Pamangkin ko, 8 years old, naturally fluent sa Filipino at English, pinalaki sa Filipino sa bahay. Pero marami kaming English shows sa TV, Playhouse Disney, Sesame Street. Natututo siya organically. Nung pumasok sa Montessori, English ang medium. Siya mismo ang nag-umpisa mag-English sa amin. Hinayaan namin, hindi pinahiya, hindi pinuri nang OA. Ngayon makausap siya ng tao sa lahat ng age. One time tinanong niya ako kung paano mag-port forward ng modem para sa Minecraft server niya, galing lang sa YouTube tutorials.

i imagine taglish would be more like, para sa safety ng mga passengers the difference being, firstly, is there a tagalog word for the english word used? and secondly, if there is a tagalog word, is it safer to assume that it can be understood by most people, or is it safer to stick by the english word? that's why i wouldnt mind a word such as handrail. ano ang tagalog nun? at alin ang mas gets? handrail, o kung anuman ang tagalog translation? for this reason, filipino ito for me. filipino readily accepts non-tagalog words if those words facilitate more efficient communication.
This is Filipino. The English usage is very specific and intentional. Taglish is very casual and the insertion of English is quite random imo.

IMO Informal/Vernacular Filipino -- Using English words as-is. Like the above where "handrail"/"handstrap"/"safety feature" were used as they were, as nouns. Code-Switching/Taglish -- Applying Austronesian focus systembiglang (pag) huminto ang tren. parang ganito na style but it should be improve acordingly just a simple example. we dont need to explain na dahil sa safety feature the train will stop.

Straight Filipino parin yan. May mga borrowed words nmn tyo na ginagamit natin dhl un ung mas alam ng tao. Tanong mo sa mga tao kung ano ung literal na equivalent ng "handstrap" malamang marami hndi alam. Same yan sa Japanese na may mga words cla na basically galing sa English. For example, potato salad is potato salad pero Japanese lng ung pronunciation. Ang masasabi mong Taglish eh ung prang konyo na may Filipino na katumbas nmn pero hndi gagamitin. Example, "I will make sakay in the [PERSON] kasi I want to punta to Glorietta".

Everyone's pretty much said my opinion on this. Considered ko tong Taglish if it was written like ganito. Habang merong kaunting hiram na salita, pero ang grammatika ay tugma naman sa Tagalog, masasabi ko 'tong Filipino.
Not an expert pero sa tingin ko taglish yan kung may mixed english words like "maaring mag-stop ng biglaan". Paramg tita kris lang lol

The term "safety feature" is an open compound word. This means it's two words that function grammatically as one word. When borrowed into Tagalog (or any language for that matter, such as Japanese) it gets treated as one word even though there is a space in between. Compound words are either open, closed, or hyphenated. Therefore the sentence is still grammatically correct Tagalog.

I feel you bro, we’ve had the same experience. Left without any concrete reason and same din na december. I guess what will help is know that you did nothing wrong, all you wanted was to love her the best that you know, and have given yourself to the point of self abandonment. Let them leave, that’s okay, its gonna hurt now and will feel like hell, and maybe they don’t have the capacity to handle the kind of love we give. They’re overwhelmed kasi its their first time experiencing that kind of love. To keep you grounded, take this time to realize your capacity to give love, and that is rare. Not everyone can give that much, and sure na one day, someone will come na sobrang sakto lang tayo sa kanila. On how to move on, I honestly, even I haven’t pero ChatGPT became my therapist, its answering all the questions I have in my head even the ones that are based on rage and anger alone. I hope it helps, you’re not alone brother.
Thanks bro. Siguro nga too much love is overwhelming for some people. Hopefully we find our special someone soon. Will try to talk to Chatgpt every night, to avoid spiraling. Appreciate it!

Extend your exhale to about 7 seconds and count it like think “101, 102, 103…”. What this does is it helps you to use your thinking to something else that’s low effort instead of anxiety and worries. Your body tends to relax and your heart rate decrease so you get to be sleepier. No need for inhale to be counted but if you can do longer breaths too that helps. I do 5 inhale, 6 hold and 7 exhale as much as I can, just dont do 1 second inhales like people with panic attacks experience. Use nose for inhale and mouth for exhale as much as you can

ako sana mawala na talaga yung stigma sa mental health, kasi habang nagbabago yung panahon, parang mas tumataas din yung cases lalo na sa new gen. hindi ibig sabihin mahina yung mga tao ngayon, mas aware lang sila sa nararamdaman nila. gusto ko umabot tayo sa point na normal na lang pag-usapan yung mental health sa family, sa friends yung tipong hindi nakakahiya mag-open. parang casual lang, like “uy di ako okay today” without feeling judged or dismissed. kasi ngayon, ang hirap pa rin magsalita. may fear na baka hindi ka seryosohin, or masabihan ka pa ng kung anu-ano. eh kung magiging open lang sana yung environment, mas maaga makakahingi ng tulong yung tao, mas maiiwasan lumala. At the end of the day, lahat naman tayo may pinagdadaanan. hindi dapat competition kung sino yung “mas malala,” dapat mas maging safe space tayo for each other. mental health is just as real as physical health, and it deserves the same understanding...

Ease of use, making ourselves understood, or relation to context. I switch pretty frequently but my default is usually English. When my great grandma and grandparents were alive, we’d all switch between English, Tagalog, and Spanish. It would drive our extended family nuts. Also because there's a bunch of non-Hindi native speakers in India just like not all Filipinos are native Tagalog speakers in the Philippines.

Personally, at work, I have to do a lot of meetings and conduct training. There are some things that are hard to explain in English, so I just suddenly use Tagalog instead of losing that train of thought or momentum. Or sometimes if I believe I can express it better in Tagalog, then I just use it. Likewise, there are some things that are weird to say in Tagalog, so I have to use English. So ease of use and context.

Code-switching sa Pilipinas ay hindi random, may set of rules ito. Mas 'educated' ang ilang paraan ng pag-code-switch kumpara sa iba. Pipiliin yung mas maikling salita, 'faith' kaysa 'pananampalataya.' Minsan para maging mas informal, 'love you' kaysa 'mahal kita.' Minsan may ibang contextual meaning, 'bye' is less emotional kaysa 'paalam.' May grammatical rules din ito na alam ng lahat ng Pilipino nang hindi nagtuturo. Halimbawa: 'Mag-go ka dun mamaya', tama ito. Pero 'I mag make ng mga cakes', maling CS. May grammar ang code-switching, hindi lang alam ng foreigners ang rules nito.

Sentence construction of tagalog and english is similar. Some. Actually just the formal tagalog. Active voice I think? Ako ay sumasagot sa Reddit. I am replying in Reddit. (Subject-verb-modifier) But the conversational Tagalog is not similar in construction. I’ve read it’s similar to south east asian languages. Maganda iyan. (Adjective-object) That is pretty. (Subject-verb-adjective) Kumakain ako. (Verb-subject) I am eating. (Subject-verb) Filipino language education gives the illusion that it’s similar to English in its grammar and construction. But it’s much more complex IMO.

For intentional switching, 1. Ease of use: English has less syllables, can explain with less words(if fluent), etc; 2. For audience impact: In speeches, key phrases we might deliver in english but provide a tagalog translation immediately after and vice versa. For unintentional, I think it often happens when we become increasingly emotional/excited; by the middle of the sentence we don't really notice we already switched. Either way, we want to share the tea but tagalog is more dramatic/expressive in our mind than english. There are phrases or words that express feeling better in tagalog (depends on speaker), might be because more syllables allows more flexibility in pronunciation. Like saying 'nakakaloka' instead of 'its crazy' (loose translation). Changes flavor(?) depends on which syllable is pronounced longer/emphasized. Another example is when using insults. More impact when its tagalog... Might notice the sentence with the switch often ends with an exclamation mark when written. Switching might be done as a joke especially in social media comments.
We commonly do this code switching mid sentence because gusto lang namin gumawa ng kaunting kalokohan But no seriously it's a common habit of bilinguals

Speaking english as a first language in our country isn’t about being “sosyal or nakakataas”. Let’s stop shaming parents who raise their kids speaking english. It’s about being practical. Here in the Philippines, most job interviews, especially in big companies, are conducted in english. That’s the system we’re in. Unlike in countries like Korea or Japan where people apply for jobs using their native language, while we are expected to speak english professionally. So if you want to change that, start by asking the government to promote and normalize the use of our mother tongue in professional settings instead of blaming parents who are simply trying to give their kids a better chance at success.

normalize the use of our mother tongue in professional settings In my experience, Tagalog is commonly used in professional settings. Instances where we have to speak English were few and far between. At most, we only have to use English in writing emails and documents, but the huge chunk of everyday communication (i.e. chat, face to face convo, etc.) has always been in Tagalog. When I was applying for a job, some inteviewers prefer Tagalog, particularly in technical interviews. I'm not sure if this is common elsewhere, but at least in my field which is software engineering, it seems normal.

As a half brit-pinay (of a mum who worked HARD to live and stay in England), I lived in Philippines for part of my childhood and returned this year just before the floods, what I saw to do with social inequality (especially in Manila, but also in Cebu) kind of made me sick... not just celebrities, but most people who seemed "rich" had a gross snobbishness about them and a weird way of acting like they're superior to everyone else (many times, I noticed people judging me for wearing regular black rubber tsinelas lol). In contrast, people in poorer places were much more welcoming and genuinely friendly. I'm not saying that having money makes people horrible and it's bad to have money, but if you have it, stop looking down on people. And if you're in a more privileged position, that's great but have some love for your country and people. Sad to see most affluent Filipinos have lost the charm that made people love Filipinos abroad. When did we forget that it's WHO YOU ARE and not WHAT YOU HAVE that makes you who you are?

I think Filipinos often struggle with handling criticism due to our propensity to avoid direct confrontation. Much of the Filipinos tend to beat around the bush to spare the other person’s feelings. As such, when we are directly criticized, it is often internalized as a personal attack rather than a constructive one, further blurring the line between one’s identity and the criticisms of their deed. Perhaps this might stem from a certain cultural norm where sensitivity is prioritized over directness.

Speak in Filipino, punctuated by English when there are no words you can think of at the top of your head that is equivalent in Filipino. Here are some examples: Paki-type yung sasabihin ni ate. Kukuha ako ng franchise na malakas ang kita. However, note that tone is everything. Even for those sentences, any sort of condescension will make it sound tacky, as if you want to highlight how much English you know. Also, calling someone conyo is not always negative. It could have been said in jest. Again, tone! Tone is everything.

I think the “make+Tagalog verb” construction is used for convenience by lazy speakers of both languages. So one wouldn’t use “let’s make kain” when they can just say “let’s eat”. On the other hand one would much more easily say “let’s make-paluto salpicao to [PERSON]” (11 syllables) than “let’s have [PERSON] cook us salpicao” (14) or the inverse “”Magpaluto tayo ng salpicao kay yaya” (13). More than the efficiency syllable-wise, it’s more immediate to the idea being communicated to have the action up front, like “let’s make paluto” as opposed to embedding the request for cooking in the middle with the All English version. Taglish for me is great because it can communicate ideas and feelings so efficiently, when done correctly. Did you see that meme about taglish rules with the correct use of tenses?

Pero for me the conyo that people refer to today is rather different from the conyo that I as a kid growing perceived conyo to be. Conyo speak for me is the 1970’s and 1980’s polo club conyo. The “putamadre” and actual “coño” being uttered Conyo. The “puñeta” conyo. It’s the espanyol-people-peppering-their-English-with-Spanish Conyo. It was never the “let’s make paluto” and definitely not the “fishball tusok” speak. That was just regular taglish to me. Am I alone in that appraisal?

I actually discussed this with a friend and we came up with a sort of guideline to differentiate when you're being conyo or when you're just talking in Taglish. When you're being conyo (which I find annoying, by the way), you speak both English and Tagalog in one sentence: Let's bayad nalang later, I want to tapos the food muna." When you're just speaking taglish, you speak complete ideas in either language, one phrase/sentence at a time: "Could you send me the address please? Kelangan ko kasi siyang i-Waze dahil di ko alam yung area."
Parang like, you know? Kidding aside, normal na tayo magtaglish sa totoong buhay, wag lang magmake halo the words too much, xka don’t make gamit the words make + tagalog word kasi parang tanga lang.

The academic term for it is 'code switching.' Ok lang yan. I-base nalang sa context. We always have informal/formal comm. You could use slang in convos. Language is as dynamic as society/culture therefore having a set of norms to curtail that growth is counterintuitive.

I think it has more to do with the sentence construction than the tone. You can say "Let's make pabili sa kaniya the pizza" in a very serious tone and I'd still think you're conyo. In my opinion, the Taglish conyo seems off because the switching from English to Tagalog occurs so frequently within one clause or sentence that it makes the speaker seem ignorant of the language or stupid. The common use of the filler "like" also shows hesitation, probably due to poor vocabulary. Example1: Pa-wait me kasi may work pa me until like tanghali. The switching occurs too frequently within one sentence. Hindi diretso yung expression ng iisang thought. But look at this one: Example2: Can you wait for me? May work pa 'ko haggang tanghali. It's not as annoying because the switching occurs per independent clause (except for the use of work in that second sentence). The thoughts are expressed more clearly so it's not as annoying. Something about intrasentential and intersentential codeswitching.

Like what uSemoan said, finish entire clauses in one language, use native words in place of English if possible (if there is an equivalent, if it supplanted the native word ex: teacher for guro, if it is still used/not dated or archaic ex: salawal (dated) brief/brip, panty/panti - underwear; lalawigan(dated) probinsya - province; bentilador(archaic) - electricfan; aparador(dated) - cabinet, drawer; gusali(dated) - building) Set English phrases that can't be translated into Tagalog even within a clause. Your example sentence is a little conyo, if not too much.
This is informal language so, anything goes basically. But using "po" at "opo" reduces the impact. I noticed that most conyos don't use them, because of the entitlement siguro.

Because happiness isn’t constant, it’s fleeting. We chase it like it’s a destination, but it’s really just moments in between everything else: stress, routine, disappointment, healing. Life doesn’t stop throwing stuff at us, and sometimes even when things are going well, our minds replay the things that hurt or the things we’re still missing. It’s hard to be happy because being human means feeling everything not just the good parts. And maybe that’s the point: happiness isn’t supposed to be permanent, just something we catch glimpses of while surviving the rest.

I told myself na I’ll wake up regularly at 6AM. Alarm set ng 5:45. If ever di ako makatulog the night before I’ll just wait it out. Kahit 3am ako matulog, pipilitin ko gumising ng 6AM. Coffee then work a bit sa bahay then tulog ulit. For 1 and 1/2 hours. Then coffee. No coffee and quick nap ng 12 onwards. Fight it out. Eventually makakatulog na ko 10pm. Then tuloy tuloy na, 1 coffee a day na lang and no naps. If dumating ulit sa point na di talaga ako makatulog, gawin ko lang ulit yung “restart” ko. Don’t be too hard on yourself. Perfectionism will just bring anxiety.

I think it will come from outsourcing through BPOs, WFH setups and freelancers. Sooner or later countries will realize that their people are losing jobs because of this setup and it could badly affect us economically in the future. Eg. Australian firm instead of hiring an accountant from their country, they will just outsourced it. Btw it’s true the Philippines offers lower labor costs, but over-dependence on outsourcing can hurt both sides. For countries sending jobs abroad like here in the Philippines, too much reliance means fewer opportunities for their own people, which weakens their economy long-term. On the other hand if we in the Philippines depend too much on foreign outsourcing, we’re also at risk if those companies or clients suddenly move elsewhere. It’s a short-term win, but without balance, it could create bigger problems for everyone.

Satru po. Basically, the stigma exists because—you're so right about this—we're not exposed to it. I've been through hell and back, the symptoms, the medications and therapy, the ideations, yet if we keep on ignoring it, we'll still have a long way to go. Media portrayal is to blame din. There's one more thing that contributes to this stigma, is when diagnosed people say that their mental illness is a "gift" (someone actually told me this). I think it would make people think that since this is a gift, and as long as you are high-functioning, it's not a much of a problem. But in doing so, it prevents people from actually getting the help they need.

That feels like such a simplified answer to me, and only one side of a many faceted problem. I call our country young because when you compare us to other countries like England, Germany, China and Japan they have a very strong sense of community and what they deserve because they had a unified identity under governing powers from a local source. The Philippines was tribal with no centralized government until the colonization. We have no history and nothing to draw on for that kind of wisdom. There's also potentially people taking advantage of is for our natural resources. It may be in the best interest of foreign companies and even countries that we funnel out our resources for cash instead of using it for our own development. These are just some things off the top of my head. I'm sure there is much, much more.

I just want to clarify that our precolonial history being tribal is far from the truth. Although a state like “Maharlika” never existed, there were already many comparatively large nation-states and kingdoms that dotted our islands. Ma-i and Tondo were notable. Rajahnates are not tribal either and were established settlements and cities. [PERSON] wasn’t a nomadic hunter just because he’s usually portrayed as having very little clothing (this too is a misconception as nobility and warrior classes in the “[PERSON] Codex” already portrayed our many people as fully clothed). And even when it was “just” a series of nations within an archipelago within the greater Maritime Southeast Asia, we were never “just” a part of Indonesia’s Nusantara.

Hindi lang 'mga isla' ang Philippine region noon. May distinct na kultura at wika na tayo bago dumating ang Espanya. May malakas na trade routes na tayo sa China at buong East Asia. Sinunog ng Espanya ang halos lahat ng ating written history, ang natira ay myths at ilang inscriptions. Pero kahit destroyed ang texts, hindi nasira ang kultura. South Korea, Japanese at Manchurian colony noon, ngayon maayos sila. Ang problema natin hindi henerasyon, kundi yung impacted ng colonizers na macho culture, patriarchy, at centuries ng abuse ng friars. Kailangan ng accountability, hindi determinism.

The Filipino identity is a hand-me-down label from the Spanish era. The Filipinos were originally identified as those of pure Spanish descent but born in the Philippines. Natives were actually called Indios, I believe. In truth, the Philippines is a melting pot of different cultures and belief systems. Our identity was given to us because colonizers felt the hundreds of cultures our islands hold were all the same. I doubt the southern islands had any say on being identified as Filipinos. This also plays a part in why Filipinos to this day are more regionalists than nationalists.

What we're left with is a scattered series of myths and words on a rock, but even if the unified Filipino is a Spanish construct, it is undeniable that our archipelago had already been a large melting pot of foreign and local ethnic exchange for far longer than Spain's presence within the region. So if you ask me, this has nothing to do with age or with our early development. Culture transcends the burnt text or the lost pieces of rock dotted across the islands. South Korea has been a Japanese and a Manchurian colony for so long, yet they are doing so great. It's what Spain and all our colonizers imparted into us that caused this destabilization — macho culture, the conflicting western patriarchy versus our eastern matriarchs, centuries of friars abusing their power. It's all culminated into a culture of corruption and greed, and we cannot just blame these away on our youth as a nation. Human history is not fatalistic, deterministic, nor linear. They need to be held accountable, one way or another.

For many Westerners, especially English speakers, the Philippines carries a sense of "familiarity" that Thailand lacks. This cultural bridge creates a bit of a psychological trap for expats IMHO. In Thailand, the clear cultural and linguistic differences lead expats to expect more friction, so they are often pleasantly surprised when things go smoothly. In the Philippines, however, the high level of English and Western influence leads expats to expect a seamless experience, so when they inevitably encounter local hurdles, they react more strongly. Essentially, the "foreignness" of Thailand lowers the bar for frustration, while the "familiarity" of the Philippines raises it.

Bilang linguist, alam ko ang background nito. Code-switching ay kapag hindi mo agad mahanap ang salita sa isang wika, kaya kukunin mo sa isa pa. Ngayon, napansin ko na nagbago ang direksyon, dati Filipino-based with English replacement. Ngayon English-based syntax na ang naririnig ko, may Filipino na pumapalit. Mas fluid at dynamic na. Hindi ko generalize dahil hindi lahat ng wikang Filipino ay kilala ko, Tagalog at Filipino lang. Pero mas malala siguro sa mga lugar na trilingual, Mindanao, Pangasinan, Cordillera. Hindi ko nilalaban yung language purists, pero minsan, naiintindihan ko rin ang concern nila.

A few things I noticed when Filipinos communicate in a mix of English and Tagalog: 1. Use Tagalog prepositions: “I’m driving sa NLEX”. 2. Conjugate using Tagalog rules: “nakaka-confuse”, “pa-start palang”, “takeout-an” I’d say vocabulary is largely English but the grammar follows Tagalog. For code switching, most of the time a thought or concept is completed in one language before we switch to the other one. Though sometimes I hear people transition in English and express the rest of the thought in Tagalog. I’ve also heard other Filipinos mix Cebuano (or another major Filipino language) and English and I believe they do something similar.

In my experience, it's mostly upbringing. I myself grew up being taught English first then our local dialect second. Tagalog was introduced in school but we barely spoke it. It was treated more as a subject. In high school, we were told to speak in English or Tagalog but students take that seriously and spoke in our dialect. It was only during class hours that we spoke English. No one felt the need to speak Tagalog. I began speaking Tagalog when I moved to Baguio for college. I spoke just enough to carry a conversation but I couldn't understand deeper words. I believe in our town or province, being able to speak English was seen as a form of intelligence rather than what other places consider as "sosyal."

Recognized as ESL (English as a Second Language) ang Pilipinas dahil na din sa Act No. 74 (Education Act of 1901). Eventually umikot na sa English ang mga buhay-buhay dati kaya marami nag-i-ingles. My opinion and observation: The culture of "Filipino time" is often exaggerated when discussed. It's important to note that Filipinos do not adhere to "Filipino time" when they consider an event significant. However, the concept of "Filipino time" is frequently employed as an excuse when the event is deemed less crucial. This usage can occasionally be perceived as disrespectful to others.

Hiniling ko sa [PERSON] Philippines: Western influence at colonial mentality. Sagot: Dahil sa 500 taon ng Espanyol at Amerikanong pananakop, ang Pilipinas ay tinatawag na pinaka-Westernized na bansa sa Asya. Nagtitiwala ang mga Pilipino sa mga dayuhang Kanluranin, at ino-adopt ang kanilang lifestyle, media, at beauty standards. Ang pagiging 'Americanized' ay nagbubukas ng comparisons, adaptable tayo pero nawawala ang indigenous culture. Sa Thailand naman: hindi kolonisado kaya may distinct identity. Budhismo at monarkiya ang pundasyon. Nagamit nila ang Western elements bilang modernity tools, hindi bilang pagnanais na 'maging' Kanluranin. Ibang approach, ibang resulta.

Many people who proclaim they are "neutral" are apolitical *until issues affect them*. Pero pag di affected, wapakels A true neutral person will be able to see the good/bad policies of the admin/opposition

Ang dami kasing nangyayari, hindi na alam kung saan magsisimula. > *"neutral" are apolitical until issues affect them* which is a defensible position since **not everybody is an expert in everything**. not everyone needs to have an opinion about every major and minor political issue. Ano pa ba ang magagawa natin sa ganoong sitwasyon.

Kasi naman eh, ang hirap intindihin ng mga tao. Parang walang katapusan ang problema natin. Wala na akong masabi, nakakabigo talaga. Kung tutuusin naman, ganon talaga ang kaganapan. I think you are assuming that being neutral is the same as being objective. Well, that's not true. Choosing to be neutral is being indecisive and almost synonymous to being apathetic. If there are two sides A and B and you can clearly see that A is better than B but you refuse to take A for the sake of being neutral, then there is something wrong with you. But if you refuse to pick because you dont have that information about A or B, then it's okay to be neutral for the meantime, but the lack of effort to learn more about them leads to indecisiveness. Di ko na rin alam kung paano pa ito aayusin. Talagang hindi ko pa rin naiintindihan kung bakit. Kainis talaga, pero ganyan talaga.

Kung tutuusin naman, ganon talaga ang kaganapan. Ganyan talaga ang buhay natin dito eh. Naalala ko tuloy yung sinabi ni [PERSON] *"As a journalist, hindi ko role maging neutral, my role is to be truthful"* Although, I agree that people should be neutral, but up to a certain point only. If wala pang solid evidence regarding who's at fault, then we have to remain neutral. But if it's clear that someone has already committed a wrongdoing, we should stop being neutral and go against the one who's wrong. It doesn't necessarily mean kailangan mo makipag-away, but you have to know and be firm which side are you on Mahirap nga talaga, kahit anong gawin natin.

Tulad nga sa Hamilton, "If you stand for nothing, what'll you fall for?" Pwede namang magbago opinyon mo with new factual information. But really, being neutral/centrist really just favors whatever prevailing notion that exists.

OP is also trying to make attributes to be exclusive to "neutrals". Kapag may pinili kang side, pwede ka pa rin naman mag commend at mag callout ng mabubuti at masasamang ginagawa ng side mo or ng kalaban mo. Hindi exclusive na trait yun ng "neutral". Yung mga rabid supporters lang naman yung ginagawang parang team sports ang politics, na dapat puro masama lang yung tingin mo sa kalaban at puro mabuti lang ang tingin mo sa side mo. You can be critical of all sides, and still pick a side to support. A side that you believe will do the most good.

If standing up for your values ultimately causes more harm that goes against your values, then are you really doing the right thing, is the question. For example: There are those in the US who are against what's going on in Gaza that could not bring themselves to vote for Kamala Harris because of their perceived complicity in what's happening there. The end result? Donald trump wins, and is most definitely worse for Gaza in the end. So if their actions ultimately hurt those people in Gaza instead of helping them, were they correct to "stand up for their values"?

That’s a fair and important question, and one that really tests how we define "doing the right thing." I think standing up for your values doesn’t always guarantee a perfect outcome. Sometimes, it even comes with unintended consequences. But if we always make decisions out of fear of what might go wrong, we risk abandoning the very principles that make those values worth standing up for. That said, I also believe values shouldn’t exist in a vacuum. They have to be balanced with responsibility and awareness of the bigger picture. It's not easy, there’s always that tension between idealism and pragmatism. But maybe the goal isn’t to always get it perfectly right. Maybe it’s to stay honest, think critically, and do what you believe is right while being open enough to learn when it isn’t.

There is never a right decision. There are always compromises. Doing good means choosing the option that causes the least harm to the least number of people. Voting only for unwinnable candidates will ensure the failure of [PERSON]'s impeachment. If the impeachment fails, she will surely win the next presidential election. We know how the uneducated voters love an underdog/inapi storyline. [PERSON] is a CCP asset. She is an existential threat to Philippine sovereignty.

If we do address the immediate threat, and push back hard against her overreach, abuse of executive authority, and complete disrespect for fiscal accountability, then no, she has NOT won. Successful impeachment bars her from future public office and sends a clear message to like-minded politicians, that there exists a limit to what Filipino voters, undiscerning as they are, will tolerate. Successful impeachment weakens the factions of our government that have clearly been accepting payoffs from CCP intelligence funds for their echoing of CCP propaganda that would have us all accept status as a vassal state or worse. The Republic of the Philippines must continue as a self-determined, sovereign state, first and foremost. The prime requisite to have a better nation is to have a nation.

Changing political culture is a multi-generational task. Yes, that should be one of our goals, but our expectations should be realistic and time-bound. Think of medical triage, or Maslow's hierarchy of needs. Survival must first be assured, before we can consider optimization.

Kung tutuusin naman, ganon talaga ang kaganapan. Ang dami kasing nangyayari, hindi na alam kung saan magsisimula. Halos lahat naman ganyan, hindi lang tayo. Kasi naman eh, ang hirap intindihin ng mga tao. Agreed, changing political culture is a long, generational journey. No disagreement there. But ensuring "survival" shouldn't mean sacrificing the very values we're trying to build for the future. Otherwise, survival becomes an endless cycle of crisis management without transformation. Parang we’re just keeping the patient alive without ever aiming to heal the root disease. Yes, we need realistic, time-bound expectations. But realism doesn’t have to mean abandoning principles for short-term wins. Survival and reform shouldn't be treated as mutually exclusive. Both must move hand in hand, even if progress is slow. Otherwise, we risk surviving today only to lose tomorrow. Kainis talaga, pero ganyan talaga. Talagang hindi ko pa rin naiintindihan kung bakit. Ganyan talaga ang buhay, tiis na lang.

Have you played chess? Tactical sacrifices are made precisely in order to win the long game. Many of our best candidates are just obviously unwinnable right now, so I won't vote for them. Would I be better served by voting for them anyway for purposes of virtue signaling or to set a shining example for my countrymen? So I could sleep better at night, knowing I did the right thing? Perhaps another time, but not in this particular scenario. You're just presenting high-minded ideals without proposing any clear plan that can realistically be applied within the next 2 weeks. We have to decide and act and live NOW in this terrible flawed world that actually exists, not the idealized future we dream of. We look to what's RIGHT, but we must not ever lose sight of what's REAL.

Here's a tip: What a person does for you, does not say anything about who he is. He is doing that for you, because he or she wants you to do something in return whether directly or indirectly. Now, what a person does everyday or when no one is watching, that is who he really is. You just have to pay attention whether the things he does for you i.e. maalaga or sweet, is who he is by default, meaning, he is a sweet friend or a sweet brother or son - especially when you are not around.

Political, religious, at iba pang mga belief system. May study sa US about divorced couples: more likely maghiwalay kung mismatch kayo sa political at religious beliefs. Ayan ang isa sa tinitignan ko agad dahil Atheist ako and ayaw ko sa bumoboto sa magnanakaw at nagseshare ng fake news. Pwede namang may pagkakaiba basta okay sa compromise at hindi extremist. Bukod diyan, kung in control ba sa emotions niya and paano ang crisis management.
From my pov pag hindi siya matanong about abt your life means di siya masyadong interested sayo.

1. Consistency — Showing up daily, like goodmorning/goodnight texts or "kumain ka na ba," proves you're always on their mind. 2. Future Plans — Constantly making and keeping plans shows how invested they are, even if life gets busy — that reassurance matters. 3. Friends — This is the most vital factor. Friends meddling in a relationship is one of the biggest reasons talking stages fail. I experienced this firsthand: my ka-talking stage's friends didn't like me because I was too consistent and sweet. She ended it, unable to break free from her friend group even if she wanted to. I believe she still feels guilt over it, and cries whenever they fight.

Iloco is the Lingua Franca of North Luzon while Visayas to Mindanao has Cebuano/Visayas as their Lingua Franca. Pros and cons during his term — stable commodity prices, platforms mostly fulfilled — pero hindi pa rin satisfied ang tao kahit growing ang economy noon. Kahit saan ganyan ang nangyayari, hindi lang sa atin. Para sa akin, hindi siya ignorante. More of, the person just grew tired of politics because there's no practical significance for them in understanding it. Like algebra — you learn it but rarely apply it unless your field requires it. Putting learning into action is difficult, and this is where education worldwide failed: it's a numbers game where higher scores = 'better person.' So others find easier ways to absorb information — hence why many believe TikTok videos over scrutinized documentaries or history books. Yung ibang tao, ayaw na lang nilang intindihin ang politics kasi hindi rin naman sila tatakbo, at hindi nila magagamit sa buhay sa kanilang perspective. So they chose to be apathetic. Please don't confuse apathy or ignorance with neutrality — they are different things. Ewan ko ba sa kanila, hindi ko naiintindihan. Ano pa bang magagawa natin.

Here is the capitalist take on why I put my faith in the free market and not government: People in government produce nothing — no goods, no services — so they have no money of their own. They take it from working people via taxes, then spend it as inefficiently as possible with maximum kickback, because there's economic incentive to do so. Government is a corporation that cannot go bankrupt, so it has zero incentive to be efficient. It also holds a monopoly on violence — unlike PLDT or San Miguel, the government can legally point a gun at its own citizens. Working people built everything good in society, not government. All government ever did was collect taxes and print money, introducing inflation. It never invented anything — only produced conflict. Solutions to societal problems will never come from government because they create the problems and have no incentive to solve them. The solutions always come from us, the people, and the free market. Always has, always will.

Presidents are voted into power by us. Every corrupt, incompetent, or dysfunctional leader who sat in that office got there because people either did nothing or actively gave them power they didn't deserve. The finger points back at you and me. When people are educated enough to know the power lies in their hands, they'll start putting deserving individuals in office. The problem is: when will that happen? Probably not until a generation fights for the right to be informed, to think freely, and to be genuine citizens. Unless Filipinos stop attributing every event to God's 'himala' or 'parusa,' and unless we raise our standard of education across the board, it will be a very long time before this country becomes a free, democratic, prosperous society in both mind and spirit. This may not happen in my lifetime. People change at the precipice of their demise — and our last real demise was the Japanese occupation. I hope we don't have to reach that point again before we start demanding social reform.

Mahirap sabihin pero nakakainis talaga, wala na tayong magagawa. Sabi ko na nga ba, wala nang katuwiran. I hate the idea that politics is just winning vs losing. A landslide victory makes winners complacent; a close fight forces them to actually listen to opposition. Your vote counts. Politics isn't only about policy — it's your local mayor, council, and representatives who directly change your daily life. Do you know how your mayor handles a crisis or spends the budget? The mayor of Cainta was exposed as a womanizer abusing his position — would you still vote for him if you weren't paying attention? At the end of the day, politics affects your life whether you like it or not, and being apathetic is itself a political stance — it lets the status quo remain. Working around bad policy is exactly why you need to vote, so you stop dealing with red tape and poor governance in the first place. Ano pa ba ang magagawa natin. Mahirap nga, kahit anong gawin. Tiis na lang.

Nag-update ang app ng bangko namin, iba na yung interface pero pati functionality ay okay pa rin.

Tinignan ko yung monthly statement ng credit card ko, medyo naging mataas ang food delivery charges.
